| | --- | | * [Inflation](/wiki/Cosmic_inflation "Cosmic inflation") **·** [Nucleosynthesis](/wiki/Big_Bang_nucleosynthesis "Big Bang nucleosynthesis") | | Backgrounds | | * [Gravitational wave (GWB)](/wiki/Gravitational_wave_background "Gravitational wave background") * [Microwave (CMB)](/wiki/Cosmic_microwave_background "Cosmic microwave background") **·** [Neutrino (CNB)](/wiki/Cosmic_neutrino_background "Cosmic neutrino background") |

comprises all of [existence](/wiki/Existence "Existence"): all forms of [matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter") and [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy"), and the structures they form, from [sub-atomic particles](/wiki/Sub-atomic_particles "Sub-atomic particles") to entire [galactic filaments](/wiki/Galaxy_filament "Galaxy filament"). Since the early 20th century, the field of [cosmology](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology") establishes that [space and time](/wiki/Space_and_time "Space and time") emerged together at the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang") 13.787±0.020 billion years ago[[9]](#cite_note-9) and that the [universe has been expanding](/wiki/Expansion_of_the_universe "Expansion of the universe") since then. The [portion of the universe that can be seen by humans](/wiki/Observable_universe "Observable universe") is approximately 93 billion [light-years](/wiki/Light-year "Light-year") in diameter at present, but the total size of the universe is not known.[[10]](#cite_note-Brian_Greene_2011-10)

The **universe** comprises all of [existence](/wiki/Existence "Existence"): all forms of [matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter") and [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy"), and the structures they form, from [sub-atomic particles](/wiki/Sub-atomic_particles "Sub-atomic particles") to entire [galactic filaments](/wiki/Galaxy_filament "Galaxy filament"). Since the early 20th century, the field of [cosmology](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology") establishes that [space and time](/wiki/Space_and_time "Space and time") emerged together at the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang") 13.787±0.020 billion years ago[[9]](#cite_note-9) and that the [universe has been expanding](/wiki/Expansion_of_the_universe "Expansion of the universe") since then. The [portion of the universe that can be seen by humans](/wiki/Observable_universe "Observable universe") is approximately 93 billion [light-years](/wiki/Light-year "Light-year") in diameter at present, but the total size of the universe is not known.[[10]](#cite_note-Brian_Greene_2011-10)

Some of the earliest [cosmological models](/wiki/Timeline_of_cosmological_theories "Timeline of cosmological theories") of the universe were [geocentric](/wiki/Geocentric_model "Geocentric model"), placing Earth at the center. During the European [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution"), astronomical observations led to [a heliocentric model](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism"). Further observational improvements led to the realization that the Sun is one of a few hundred billion stars in the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way"), which is one of a few hundred billion galaxies in the observable universe. [At the largest scale](/wiki/End_of_Greatness "End of Greatness"), galaxies are distributed uniformly and the same in all directions, meaning that the universe has no edge. The universe is also infinitely large, meaning it has no center. At smaller scales, galaxies are distributed in [clusters](/wiki/Galaxy_cluster "Galaxy cluster") and [superclusters](/wiki/Supercluster "Supercluster"), which form immense filaments and [voids](/wiki/Void_(astronomy) "Void (astronomy)") in space, creating a vast foam-like structure.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Discoveries in the early 20th century led to the Big Bang theory, which describes a with a hot fireball cooling and becoming less dense as the universe expanded, allowing the first [subatomic particles](/wiki/Subatomic_particle "Subatomic particle") and simple [atoms](/wiki/Atom "Atom") to form. Giant clouds of [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") and [helium](/wiki/Helium "Helium") were gradually drawn to the places where matter was most [dense](/wiki/Density "Density"), forming the first galaxies, stars, and eventually, everything else.

From studying the effects of [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity") on both matter and light, it has been discovered that the universe contains much more matter than is accounted for by visible objects; stars, galaxies, nebulae and interstellar gas. This unseen matter is known as [dark matter](/wiki/Dark_matter "Dark matter").[[12]](#cite_note-12) In the widely accepted [ΛCDM](/wiki/Lambda-CDM_model "Lambda-CDM model") cosmological model, dark matter accounts for about 25.8%±1.1% of the mass and energy in the universe while about 69.2%±1.2% is [dark energy](/wiki/Dark_energy "Dark energy"), a mysterious form of energy responsible for the [acceleration](/wiki/Accelerated_expansion "Accelerated expansion") of the expansion of the universe.[[13]](#cite_note-planck_2015-13) Ordinary ('[baryonic](/wiki/Baryon#Baryonic_matter "Baryon")') matter therefore composes only 4.84%±0.1% of the universe.[[13]](#cite_note-planck_2015-13) Stars, planets, and visible gas clouds only form about 6% of this ordinary matter.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

There are many competing hypotheses about the [ultimate fate of the universe](/wiki/Ultimate_fate_of_the_universe "Ultimate fate of the universe") and about what, if anything, preceded the Big Bang.

## Definition

[Hubble Space Telescope](/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope "Hubble Space Telescope") – [Ultra-Deep Field galaxies](/wiki/Hubble_Ultra-Deep_Field "Hubble Ultra-Deep Field") to Legacy field zoom out  
(video 00:50; May 2, 2019)

The physical universe has been defined as "The totality of all space and time; all that is, has been, and will be."[[15]](#cite_note-Zeilik1998-15) The universe contains all energy and [matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter"),[[16]](#cite_note-Britannica-16) including therefore planets, [moons](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite"), stars, galaxies, and the contents of [intergalactic space](/wiki/Intergalactic_space "Intergalactic space").[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Some philosophers and scientists support the inclusion of ideas and abstract concepts—such as mathematics and logic—in the definition of the universe.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) The word *universe* may also refer to concepts such as *the [cosmos](/wiki/Cosmos "Cosmos")*, *the [world](/wiki/World "World")*, and *[nature](/wiki/Nature "Nature")*.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Bolonkin2011-24)

## Etymology

The word *universe* derives from the [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") word *univers*, which in turn derives from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word *universus*, meaning 'combined into one'.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The Latin word 'universum' was used by [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero") and later Latin authors in many of the same senses as the modern [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") word is used.[[26]](#cite_note-lewis_short-26)

### Synonyms

A term for *universe* among the ancient Greek philosophers from [Pythagoras](/wiki/Pythagoras "Pythagoras") onwards was τὸ πᾶν (*tò pân*) 'the all', defined as all matter and all space, and τὸ ὅλον (*tò hólon*) 'all things', which did not necessarily include the void.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) Another synonym was ὁ κόσμος (*ho kósmos*) meaning 'the [world](/wiki/World_(philosophy) "World (philosophy)"), the [cosmos](/wiki/Cosmos "Cosmos")'.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Synonyms are also found in Latin authors (*totum*, *mundus*, *natura*)[[30]](#cite_note-30) and survive in modern languages, e.g., the [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") words *Das All*, *Weltall*, and *Natur* for *universe*. The same synonyms are found in English, such as everything (as in the [theory of everything](/wiki/Theory_of_everything "Theory of everything")), the cosmos (as in [cosmology](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology")), the world (as in the [many-worlds interpretation](/wiki/Many-worlds_interpretation "Many-worlds interpretation")), and [nature](/wiki/Nature "Nature") (as in [natural laws](/wiki/Natural_law "Natural law") or [natural philosophy](/wiki/Natural_philosophy "Natural philosophy")).[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The prevailing model for the evolution of the universe is the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang") theory.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) The Big Bang model states that the earliest state of the universe was an extremely hot and dense one, and that the universe subsequently expanded and cooled. The model is based on [general relativity](/wiki/General_relativity "General relativity") and on simplifying assumptions such as the [homogeneity](/wiki/Homogeneity_(physics)#Translation_invariance "Homogeneity (physics)") and [isotropy](/wiki/Isotropy "Isotropy") of space. A version of the model with a [cosmological constant](/wiki/Cosmological_constant "Cosmological constant") (Lambda) and [cold dark matter](/wiki/Cold_dark_matter "Cold dark matter"), known as the [Lambda-CDM model](/wiki/Lambda-CDM_model "Lambda-CDM model"), is the simplest model that provides a reasonably good account of various observations about the universe.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/CMB_Timeline300_no_WMAP.jpg/500px-CMB_Timeline300_no_WMAP.jpg)](/wiki/File:CMB_Timeline300_no_WMAP.jpg)

In this schematic diagram, time passes from left to right, with the universe represented by a disk-shaped "slice" at any given time. Time and size are not to scale. To make the early stages visible, the time to the afterglow stage (really the first 0.003%) is stretched and the subsequent expansion (really by 1,100 times to the present) is largely suppressed.

The initial hot, dense state is called the [Planck epoch](/wiki/Planck_epoch "Planck epoch"), a brief period extending from time zero to one [Planck time](/wiki/Planck_time "Planck time") unit of approximately 10−43 seconds. During the Planck epoch, all types of matter and all types of energy were concentrated into a dense state, and [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity")—currently the weakest by far of the [four known forces](/wiki/Fundamental_interactions "Fundamental interactions")—is believed to have been as strong as the other fundamental forces, and all the forces may have been [unified](/wiki/Grand_unification "Grand unification"). The physics controlling this very early period (including [quantum gravity](/wiki/Quantum_gravity "Quantum gravity") in the Planck epoch) is not understood, so we cannot say what, if anything, happened [before time zero](/wiki/Big_Bang#Pre–Big_Bang_cosmology "Big Bang"). Since the Planck epoch, [the universe has been expanding](/wiki/Expansion_of_the_universe "Expansion of the universe") to its present scale, with a very short but intense period of [cosmic inflation](/wiki/Cosmic_inflation "Cosmic inflation") speculated to have occurred within the first [10−32](/wiki/Scientific_Notation "Scientific Notation") seconds.[[34]](#cite_note-Sivaram-34) This initial period of inflation would explain why space appears to be [very flat](/wiki/Flatness_problem "Flatness problem").

Within the first fraction of a second of the universe's existence, the four fundamental forces had separated. As the universe continued to cool from its inconceivably hot state, various types of [elementary particles](/wiki/Elementary_particle "Elementary particle") associated stably into ever larger combinations, including stable [protons](/wiki/Proton "Proton") and [neutrons](/wiki/Neutron "Neutron"), which then formed more complex [atomic nuclei](/wiki/Atomic_nuclei "Atomic nuclei") through [nuclear fusion](/wiki/Nuclear_fusion "Nuclear fusion").[[35]](#cite_note-Johnson_474–478-35)[[36]](#cite_note-durrer-36)

This process, known as [Big Bang nucleosynthesis](/wiki/Big_Bang_nucleosynthesis "Big Bang nucleosynthesis"), lasted for about 17 minutes and ended about 20 minutes after the Big Bang, so only the fastest and simplest reactions occurred. About 25% of the [protons](/wiki/Proton "Proton") and all the [neutrons](/wiki/Neutron "Neutron") in the universe, by mass, were converted to [helium](/wiki/Helium "Helium"), with small amounts of [deuterium](/wiki/Deuterium "Deuterium") (a [form](/wiki/Isotope "Isotope") of [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen")) and traces of [lithium](/wiki/Lithium "Lithium"). Any other [element](/wiki/Chemical_element "Chemical element") was only formed in very tiny quantities. The other 75% of the protons remained unaffected, as [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") nuclei.[[35]](#cite_note-Johnson_474–478-35)[[36]](#cite_note-durrer-36): 27–42

After nucleosynthesis ended, the universe entered a period known as the [photon epoch](/wiki/Photon_epoch "Photon epoch"). During this period, the universe was still far too hot for matter to form neutral [atoms](/wiki/Atom "Atom"), so it contained a hot, dense, foggy [plasma](/wiki/Plasma_(physics) "Plasma (physics)") of negatively charged [electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron"), neutral [neutrinos](/wiki/Neutrino "Neutrino") and positive nuclei. After about 377,000 years, the universe had cooled enough that electrons and nuclei could form the first stable [atoms](/wiki/Atom "Atom"). This is known as [recombination](/wiki/Recombination_(cosmology) "Recombination (cosmology)") for historical reasons; electrons and nuclei were combining for the first time. Unlike plasma, neutral atoms are [transparent](/wiki/Opacity_(optics) "Opacity (optics)") to many [wavelengths](/wiki/Wavelength "Wavelength") of light, so for the first time, the universe also became transparent. The photons released ("[decoupled](/wiki/Photon_decoupling "Photon decoupling")") when these atoms formed can still be seen today; they form the [cosmic microwave background](/wiki/Cosmic_microwave_background "Cosmic microwave background") (CMB).[[36]](#cite_note-durrer-36): 15–27

As the universe expands, the [energy density](/wiki/Energy_density "Energy density") of [electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") decreases more quickly than does that of [matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter") because the energy of each photon decreases as it is [cosmologically redshifted](/wiki/Cosmological_redshift "Cosmological redshift"). At around 47,000 years, the [energy density](/wiki/Energy_density "Energy density") of matter became larger than that of photons and [neutrinos](/wiki/Neutrino "Neutrino"), and began to dominate the large scale behavior of the universe. This marked the end of the [radiation-dominated era](/wiki/Radiation-dominated_era "Radiation-dominated era") and the start of the [matter-dominated era](/wiki/Matter-dominated_era "Matter-dominated era").[[37]](#cite_note-steane-37): 390

In the earliest stages of the universe, tiny fluctuations within the universe's density led to [concentrations](/wiki/Filament_(cosmology) "Filament (cosmology)") of [dark matter](/wiki/Dark_matter "Dark matter") gradually forming. Ordinary matter, attracted to these by [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity"), formed large gas clouds and eventually, stars and galaxies, where the dark matter was most dense, and [voids](/wiki/Void_(astronomy) "Void (astronomy)") where it was least dense. After around 100–300 million years,[[37]](#cite_note-steane-37): 333 the first [stars](/wiki/Star "Star") formed, known as [Population III](/wiki/Population_III "Population III") stars. These were probably very massive, luminous, [non metallic](/wiki/Metallicity "Metallicity") and short-lived. They were responsible for the gradual [reionization](/wiki/Reionization "Reionization") of the universe between about 200–500 million years and 1 billion years, and also for seeding the universe with elements heavier than helium, through [stellar nucleosynthesis](/wiki/Stellar_nucleosynthesis "Stellar nucleosynthesis").[[38]](#cite_note-38)

The universe also contains a mysterious energy—possibly a [scalar field](/wiki/Scalar_field "Scalar field")—called [dark energy](/wiki/Dark_energy "Dark energy"), the density of which does not change over time. After about 9.8 billion years, the universe had expanded sufficiently so that the density of matter was less than the density of dark energy, marking the beginning of the present [dark-energy-dominated era](/wiki/Dark-energy-dominated_era "Dark-energy-dominated era").[[39]](#cite_note-39) In this era, the expansion of the universe is [accelerating](/wiki/Accelerating_expansion_of_the_universe "Accelerating expansion of the universe") due to dark energy.

## Physical properties

Main articles: [Observable universe](/wiki/Observable_universe "Observable universe"), [Age of the universe](/wiki/Age_of_the_universe "Age of the universe"), and [Expansion of the universe](/wiki/Expansion_of_the_universe "Expansion of the universe")

Of the four [fundamental interactions](/wiki/Fundamental_interaction "Fundamental interaction"), [gravitation](/wiki/Gravitation "Gravitation") is the dominant at astronomical length scales. Gravity's effects are cumulative; by contrast, the effects of positive and negative charges tend to cancel one another, making electromagnetism relatively insignificant on astronomical length scales. The remaining two interactions, the [weak](/wiki/Weak_nuclear_force "Weak nuclear force") and [strong nuclear forces](/wiki/Strong_nuclear_force "Strong nuclear force"), decline very rapidly with distance; their effects are confined mainly to sub-atomic length scales.[[40]](#cite_note-OpenStax-college-physics-40): 1470

### Size and regions

See also: [Observational cosmology](/wiki/Observational_cosmology "Observational cosmology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Extended_universe_logarithmic_illustration_%28English_annotated%29.png/960px-Extended_universe_logarithmic_illustration_%28English_annotated%29.png)](/wiki/File:Extended_universe_logarithmic_illustration_(English_annotated).png)

Illustration of the observable universe, centered on the Sun. The distance scale is [logarithmic](/wiki/Logarithmic_scale "Logarithmic scale"). Due to the finite speed of light, we see more distant parts of the universe at earlier times.

Due to the finite [speed of light](/wiki/Speed_of_light "Speed of light"), there is a limit (known as the [particle horizon](/wiki/Particle_horizon "Particle horizon")) to how far light can travel over the [age of the universe](/wiki/Age_of_the_universe "Age of the universe").
The spatial region from which we can receive light is called the [observable universe](/wiki/Observable_universe "Observable universe"). The [proper distance](/wiki/Comoving_distance "Comoving distance") (measured at a fixed time) between Earth and the edge of the observable universe is 46 billion light-years[[41]](#cite_note-Extra_Dimensions_in_Space_and_Time-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) (14 billion [parsecs](/wiki/Parsecs "Parsecs")), making the [diameter of the observable universe](/wiki/Observable_universe#Size "Observable universe") about 93 billion light-years (28 billion parsecs).[[41]](#cite_note-Extra_Dimensions_in_Space_and_Time-41) Although the distance traveled by light from the edge of the observable universe is close to the [age of the universe](/wiki/Age_of_the_universe "Age of the universe") times the speed of light, 13.8 billion light-years (4.2×10~~^~~9 pc), the proper distance is larger because the edge of the observable universe and the Earth have since moved further apart.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

For comparison, the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") is roughly 87,400 light-years in diameter,[[44]](#cite_note-Goodwin-44) and the nearest sister galaxy to the Milky Way, the [Andromeda Galaxy](/wiki/Andromeda_Galaxy "Andromeda Galaxy"), is located roughly 2.5 million light-years away.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Because humans cannot observe space beyond the edge of the observable universe, it is unknown whether the size of the universe in its totality is finite or infinite.[[10]](#cite_note-Brian_Greene_2011-10)[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) An estimate from 2011 suggests that if the [cosmological principle](/wiki/Cosmological_principle "Cosmological principle") holds, the whole universe must be more than 250 times larger than a [Hubble sphere](/wiki/Hubble_volume "Hubble volume").[[48]](#cite_note-48) Some disputed[[49]](#cite_note-49) estimates for the total size of the universe, if finite, reach as high as ![{\displaystyle 10^{10^{10^{122}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f0da4c6feb5287d705dc0f51c21317c5ddbce34b) megaparsecs, as implied by a suggested resolution of the [No-Boundary Proposal](/wiki/Hartle%E2%80%93Hawking_state "Hartle–Hawking state").[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[a]](#cite_note-bignumber-51)

### Age and expansion

Main articles: [Age of the universe](/wiki/Age_of_the_universe "Age of the universe") and [Expansion of the universe](/wiki/Expansion_of_the_universe "Expansion of the universe")

Assuming that the [Lambda-CDM model](/wiki/Lambda-CDM_model "Lambda-CDM model") is correct, the measurements of the parameters using a variety of techniques by numerous experiments yield a best value of the age of the universe at 13.799 [±](/wiki/Measurement_uncertainty "Measurement uncertainty") 0.021 billion years, as of 2015.[[2]](#cite_note-Planck_2015-2)

Over time, the universe and its contents have evolved. For example, the relative population of [quasars](/wiki/Quasar "Quasar") and galaxies has changed[[51]](#cite_note-52) and the [universe has expanded](/wiki/Expansion_of_the_universe "Expansion of the universe"). This expansion is inferred from the observation that the light from distant galaxies has been [redshifted](/wiki/Redshift "Redshift"), which implies that the galaxies are receding from us. Analyses of [Type Ia supernovae](/wiki/Type_Ia_supernova "Type Ia supernova") indicate that the [expansion is accelerating](/wiki/Accelerating_expansion_of_the_Universe "Accelerating expansion of the Universe").[[52]](#cite_note-riess-53)[[53]](#cite_note-perlmutter-54)

The more matter there is in the universe, the stronger the mutual [gravitational](/wiki/Gravitational "Gravitational") pull of the matter. If the universe were *too* dense then it would re-collapse into a [black hole](/wiki/Black_hole "Black hole"). However, if the universe contained too *little* matter then it would expand too quickly for astronomical structures, like galaxies or planets, to form.[[54]](#cite_note-55) Since the Big Bang, the universe has expanded [monotonically](/wiki/Monotonic "Monotonic"). The [mass–energy density](/wiki/Critical_Mass_Density_of_the_Universe "Critical Mass Density of the Universe") of the universe, equivalent to about 5 protons per cubic meter, allowed it to expand for the last 13.8 billion years, giving time to form the universe as observed today.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

There are dynamical forces acting on the particles in the universe which affect the expansion rate. Before 1998, it was expected that the expansion rate would be decreasing as time went on due to the influence of gravitational interactions in the universe; and thus there is an additional observable quantity in the universe called the [deceleration parameter](/wiki/Deceleration_parameter "Deceleration parameter"), which most cosmologists expected to be positive and related to the matter density of the universe. In 1998, the deceleration parameter was measured by two different groups to be negative, approximately −0.55, which technically implies that the second derivative of the cosmic [scale factor](/wiki/Scale_factor_cosmology "Scale factor cosmology") ![{\displaystyle {\ddot {a}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6b4d8db669dea9bee5d348bbecf6434914a406ac) has been positive in the last 5–6 billion years.[[56]](#cite_note-nobel_2011-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58)

### Spacetime

Main articles: [Spacetime](/wiki/Spacetime "Spacetime") and [World line](/wiki/World_line "World line")

See also: [Lorentz transformation](/wiki/Lorentz_transformation "Lorentz transformation")

Modern physics regards [events](/wiki/Event_(relativity) "Event (relativity)") as being organized into [spacetime](/wiki/Spacetime "Spacetime").[[58]](#cite_note-59) This idea originated with the [special theory of relativity](/wiki/Special_theory_of_relativity "Special theory of relativity"), which predicts that if one observer sees two events happening in different places at the same time, a second observer who is moving relative to the first will see those events happening at different times.[[59]](#cite_note-Mermin2005-60): 45–52 The two observers will disagree on the time ![{\displaystyle T}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ec7200acd984a1d3a3d7dc455e262fbe54f7f6e0) between the events, and they will disagree about the distance ![{\displaystyle D}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f34a0c600395e5d4345287e21fb26efd386990e6) separating the events, but they will agree on the [speed of light](/wiki/Speed_of_light "Speed of light") ![{\displaystyle c}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/86a67b81c2de995bd608d5b2df50cd8cd7d92455), and they will measure the same value for the combination ![{\displaystyle c^{2}T^{2}-D^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1ed8e3195696a8241125fc70afb09b9ce15c3ae4).[[59]](#cite_note-Mermin2005-60): 80 The square root of the [absolute value](/wiki/Absolute_value "Absolute value") of this quantity is called the *interval* between the two events. The interval expresses how widely separated events are, not just in space or in time, but in the combined setting of spacetime.[[59]](#cite_note-Mermin2005-60): 84, 136[[60]](#cite_note-61)

The special theory of relativity describes a flat spacetime. Its successor, the [general theory of relativity](/wiki/General_theory_of_relativity "General theory of relativity"), explains [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity") as curvature of [spacetime](/wiki/Spacetime "Spacetime") arising due to its energy content. A curved path like an orbit is not the result of a force deflecting a body from an ideal straight-line path, but rather the body's attempt to fall freely through a background that is itself curved by the presence of other masses. A remark by [John Archibald Wheeler](/wiki/John_Archibald_Wheeler "John Archibald Wheeler") that has become proverbial among physicists summarizes the theory: "Spacetime tells matter how to move; matter tells spacetime how to curve",[[61]](#cite_note-Wheeler-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63) and therefore there is no point in considering one without the other.[[63]](#cite_note-Hawking-64) The [Newtonian theory of gravity](/wiki/Newton%27s_law_of_universal_gravitation "Newton's law of universal gravitation") is a good approximation to the predictions of general relativity when gravitational effects are weak and objects are moving slowly compared to the speed of light.[[64]](#cite_note-65): 327[[65]](#cite_note-66)

The relation between matter distribution and spacetime curvature is given by the [Einstein field equations](/wiki/Einstein_field_equations "Einstein field equations"), which require [tensor calculus](/wiki/Tensor_calculus "Tensor calculus") to express.[[66]](#cite_note-67): 43[[67]](#cite_note-68) The universe appears to be a smooth spacetime continuum consisting of three [spatial](/wiki/Space "Space") [dimensions](/wiki/Dimension "Dimension") and one temporal ([time](/wiki/Time "Time")) dimension. Therefore, an event in the spacetime of the physical universe can be identified by a set of four coordinates: (*x*, *y*, *z*, *t*).

### Shape

Main article: [Shape of the universe](/wiki/Shape_of_the_universe "Shape of the universe")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/End_of_universe.jpg/250px-End_of_universe.jpg)](/wiki/File:End_of_universe.jpg)

The three possible options for the shape of the universe

Cosmologists often work with [space-like](/wiki/Space-like "Space-like") slices of spacetime that are surfaces of constant time in [comoving coordinates](/wiki/Comoving_distance "Comoving distance"). The geometry of these spatial slices is set by the [density parameter](/wiki/Density_parameter "Density parameter"), Omega (Ω), defined as the average matter density of the universe divided by a critical value. This selects one of three possible [geometries](/wiki/Shape_of_the_universe "Shape of the universe") depending on whether Ω is equal to, less than, or greater than 1. These are called, respectively, the flat, open and closed universes.[[68]](#cite_note-FateOfTheUniverse-69)

Observations, including the [Cosmic Background Explorer](/wiki/Cosmic_Background_Explorer "Cosmic Background Explorer") (COBE), [Wilkinson Microwave Anisotropy Probe](/wiki/Wilkinson_Microwave_Anisotropy_Probe "Wilkinson Microwave Anisotropy Probe") (WMAP), and [Planck](/wiki/Planck_(spacecraft) "Planck (spacecraft)") maps of the CMB, suggest that the universe is infinite in extent with a finite age, as described by the [Friedmann–Lemaître–Robertson–Walker](/wiki/Friedmann%E2%80%93Lema%C3%AEtre%E2%80%93Robertson%E2%80%93Walker_metric "Friedmann–Lemaître–Robertson–Walker metric") (FLRW) models.[[69]](#cite_note-nasa_popular_uni_curv-70)[[70]](#cite_note-Nat03-71)[[71]](#cite_note-RBSG08-72)[[72]](#cite_note-Aurich0403597-73) These FLRW models thus support inflationary models and the standard model of cosmology, describing a [flat](/wiki/Minkowski_space "Minkowski space"), homogeneous universe presently dominated by [dark matter](/wiki/Dark_matter "Dark matter") and [dark energy](/wiki/Dark_energy "Dark energy").[[73]](#cite_note-planck_cosmological_parameters-74)[[74]](#cite_note-75)

### Support of life

Main article: [Fine-tuned universe](/wiki/Fine-tuned_universe "Fine-tuned universe")

The fine-tuned universe hypothesis is the proposition that the conditions that allow the existence of observable [life](/wiki/Life "Life") in the universe can only occur when certain universal [fundamental physical constants](/wiki/Physical_constant "Physical constant") lie within a very narrow range of values. According to this hypothesis, if any of several fundamental constants were only slightly different, the universe would have been unlikely to be conducive to the establishment and development of [matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter"), astronomical structures, elemental diversity, or life as it is understood. Whether this is true, and whether that question is even logically meaningful to ask, are subjects of much debate.[[75]](#cite_note-stanford_encylopedia-76) The proposition is discussed among [philosophers](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy"), [scientists](/wiki/Scientist "Scientist"), [theologians](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), and proponents of [creationism](/wiki/Creationism "Creationism").[[76]](#cite_note-toa-77)

## Composition

See also: [Galaxy formation and evolution](/wiki/Galaxy_formation_and_evolution "Galaxy formation and evolution"), [Galaxy cluster](/wiki/Galaxy_cluster "Galaxy cluster"), and [Nebula](/wiki/Nebula "Nebula")

The [mass–energy](/wiki/Mass%E2%80%93energy_equivalence "Mass–energy equivalence") density of the universe is 68% dark energy, 27% dark matter, and 5% [ordinary matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter"). Other contents are [neutrinos](/wiki/Neutrino "Neutrino") (less than 0.3%) and [electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") (about 0.005%).[[77]](#cite_note-Baumann-2022-78): 57
The universe has 10 billion times more [matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter") than [antimatter](/wiki/Antimatter "Antimatter").[[77]](#cite_note-Baumann-2022-78): 6 In the very early universe matter and antimatter annihilated each other leaving a high density of [photons](/wiki/Photon "Photon"). In the [standard model of particle physics](/wiki/Standard_model_of_particle_physics "Standard model of particle physics"), equal amounts antimatter and matter should have been [created](/wiki/Baryogenesis "Baryogenesis"). The cause of this observed [baryon asymmetry](/wiki/Baryon_asymmetry "Baryon asymmetry") is not known.[[78]](#cite_note-79): 300[[79]](#cite_note-NAT-20171020-80)

The distribution of matter throughout the universe is highly variable. The average density is about 1 proton per 200 litres. Vast volumes of the universe are [voids](/wiki/Void_(astronomy) "Void (astronomy)") of exceptionally low density. The [interstellar medium](/wiki/Interstellar_medium "Interstellar medium") far from stars but within a galaxy has density of a few protons per litre.[[80]](#cite_note-81): 57

The proportions of all types of matter and energy have changed over the history of the universe.[[81]](#cite_note-82) The total amount of electromagnetic radiation generated within the universe has decreased by 1/2 in the past 2 billion years.[[82]](#cite_note-83)[[83]](#cite_note-84) Today, ordinary matter, which includes atoms, stars, galaxies, and [life](/wiki/Life "Life"), accounts for only 4.9% of the contents of the universe.[[7]](#cite_note-planck2013parameters-7) The present overall [density](/wiki/Density "Density") of this type of matter is very low, roughly 4.5 × 10−31 grams per cubic centimeter, corresponding to a density of the order of only one proton for every four cubic meters of volume.[[4]](#cite_note-wmap_universe_made_of-4) The nature of both dark energy and dark matter is unknown. Dark matter, a mysterious form of matter that has not yet been identified, accounts for 26.8% of the cosmic contents. Dark energy, which is the energy of empty space and is causing the expansion of the universe to accelerate, accounts for the remaining 68.3% of the contents.[[7]](#cite_note-planck2013parameters-7)[[84]](#cite_note-DarkMatter-85)[[85]](#cite_note-peebles-86)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Formation_of_galactic_clusters_and_filaments.jpg/960px-Formation_of_galactic_clusters_and_filaments.jpg)](/wiki/File:Formation_of_galactic_clusters_and_filaments.jpg)

The formation of clusters and large-scale [filaments](/wiki/Galaxy_filament "Galaxy filament") in the [cold dark matter](/wiki/Cold_dark_matter "Cold dark matter") model with [dark energy](/wiki/Dark_energy "Dark energy"). The frames show the evolution of structures in a 43 million parsecs (or 140 million light-years) box from redshift of 30 to the present epoch (upper left z=30 to lower right z=0).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d8/Nearsc.gif)](/wiki/File:Nearsc.gif)

A map of the superclusters and [voids](/wiki/Void_(astronomy) "Void (astronomy)") nearest to Earth

Matter, dark matter, and dark energy are distributed homogeneously throughout the universe over length scales longer than 300 million light-years (ly) or so.[[86]](#cite_note-87) However, over shorter length-scales, matter tends to clump hierarchically; many [atoms](/wiki/Atom "Atom") are condensed into [stars](/wiki/Star "Star"), most stars into galaxies, most galaxies into [clusters, superclusters](/wiki/Galaxy_groups_and_clusters "Galaxy groups and clusters") and, finally, large-scale [galactic filaments](/wiki/Galaxy_filament "Galaxy filament"). The observable universe contains as many as an estimated 2 trillion galaxies[[87]](#cite_note-BBC-20231129-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89)[[89]](#cite_note-90) and, overall, as many as an estimated 1024 stars[[90]](#cite_note-ESA-2019-91)[[91]](#cite_note-92) – more stars (and earth-like planets) than all the [grains of beach sand](/wiki/Sand "Sand") on planet [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth");[[92]](#cite_note-SU-20020201-93)[[93]](#cite_note-CNET-20150319-94)[[94]](#cite_note-MNRAS-20150313-95) but less than the total number of atoms estimated in the universe as 1082;[[95]](#cite_note-LS-20210711-96) and the estimated total number of stars in an [inflationary universe](/wiki/Inflation_(cosmology) "Inflation (cosmology)") (observed and unobserved), as 10100.[[96]](#cite_note-SR-20200203-97) Typical galaxies range from [dwarfs](/wiki/Dwarf_galaxy "Dwarf galaxy") with as few as ten million[[97]](#cite_note-98) (107) stars up to giants with one [trillion](/wiki/10%5E12 "10^12")[[98]](#cite_note-M101-99) (1012) stars. Between the larger structures are [voids](/wiki/Void_(astronomy) "Void (astronomy)"), which are typically 10–150 Mpc (33 million–490 million ly) in diameter. The [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") is in the [Local Group](/wiki/Local_Group "Local Group") of galaxies, which in turn is in the [Laniakea Supercluster](/wiki/Laniakea_Supercluster "Laniakea Supercluster").[[99]](#cite_note-:0-100) This supercluster spans over 500 million light-years, while the Local Group spans over 10 million light-years.[[100]](#cite_note-101) The universe also has vast regions of relative emptiness; the largest known void measures 1.8 billion ly (550 Mpc) across.[[101]](#cite_note-102)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Universe_content_bar_chart.svg/500px-Universe_content_bar_chart.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Universe_content_bar_chart.svg)

Comparison of the contents of the universe today to 380,000 years after the Big Bang, as measured with 5 year WMAP data (from 2008).[[102]](#cite_note-103) Due to rounding, the sum of these numbers is not 100%.

The observable universe is [isotropic](/wiki/Isotropic "Isotropic") on scales significantly larger than superclusters, meaning that the statistical properties of the universe are the same in all directions as observed from Earth. The universe is bathed in highly isotropic [microwave](/wiki/Microwave "Microwave") [radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") that corresponds to a [thermal equilibrium](/wiki/Thermal_equilibrium "Thermal equilibrium") [blackbody spectrum](/wiki/Blackbody_spectrum "Blackbody spectrum") of roughly 2.72548 [kelvins](/wiki/Kelvin "Kelvin").[[6]](#cite_note-Fixsen-6) The hypothesis that the large-scale universe is homogeneous and isotropic is known as the [cosmological principle](/wiki/Cosmological_principle "Cosmological principle").[[103]](#cite_note-104) A universe that is both homogeneous and isotropic looks the same from all vantage points and has no center.[[104]](#cite_note-Liddle-105)[[105]](#cite_note-livio-106)

### Dark energy

Main article: [Dark energy](/wiki/Dark_energy "Dark energy")

An explanation for why the expansion of the universe is accelerating remains elusive. It is often attributed to the gravitational influence of "dark energy", an unknown form of energy that is hypothesized to permeate space.[[106]](#cite_note-peebles(a)-107) On a [mass–energy equivalence](/wiki/Mass%E2%80%93energy_equivalence "Mass–energy equivalence") basis, the density of dark energy (~ 7 × 10−30 g/cm3) is much less than the density of ordinary matter or dark matter within galaxies. However, in the present dark-energy era, it dominates the mass–energy of the universe because it is uniform across space.[[107]](#cite_note-108)[[108]](#cite_note-109)

Two proposed forms for dark energy are the [cosmological constant](/wiki/Cosmological_constant "Cosmological constant"), a *constant* energy density filling space homogeneously,[[109]](#cite_note-carroll-110) and [scalar fields](/wiki/Scalar_field "Scalar field") such as [quintessence](/wiki/Quintessence_(physics) "Quintessence (physics)") or [moduli](/wiki/Moduli_(physics) "Moduli (physics)"), *dynamic* quantities whose energy density can vary in time and space while still permeating them enough to cause the observed rate of expansion. Contributions from scalar fields that are constant in space are usually also included in the cosmological constant. The cosmological constant can be formulated to be equivalent to [vacuum energy](/wiki/Vacuum_energy "Vacuum energy").

### Dark matter

Main article: [Dark matter](/wiki/Dark_matter "Dark matter")

Dark matter is a hypothetical kind of [matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter") that is invisible to the entire [electromagnetic spectrum](/wiki/Electromagnetic_spectrum "Electromagnetic spectrum"), but which accounts for most of the matter in the universe. The existence and properties of dark matter are inferred from its gravitational effects on visible matter, radiation, and the [large-scale structure](/wiki/Observable_universe#Large-scale_structure "Observable universe") of the universe. Other than [neutrinos](/wiki/Neutrinos "Neutrinos"), a form of [hot dark matter](/wiki/Hot_dark_matter "Hot dark matter"), dark matter has not been detected directly, making it one of the greatest mysteries in modern [astrophysics](/wiki/Astrophysics "Astrophysics"). Dark matter neither [emits](/wiki/Blackbody_spectrum "Blackbody spectrum") nor absorbs light or any other [electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") at any significant level. Dark matter is estimated to constitute 26.8% of the total mass–energy and 84.5% of the total matter in the universe.[[84]](#cite_note-DarkMatter-85)[[110]](#cite_note-planckcam-111)

### Ordinary matter

Main article: [Matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Masses_and_sizes_of_objects_in_our_Universe.png/250px-Masses_and_sizes_of_objects_in_our_Universe.png)](/wiki/File:Masses_and_sizes_of_objects_in_our_Universe.png)

Masses and sizes of objects in the Universe[[111]](#cite_note-112)

The remaining 4.9% of the mass–energy of the universe is ordinary matter, that is, [atoms](/wiki/Atom "Atom"), [ions](/wiki/Ion "Ion"), [electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron") and the objects they form. This matter includes [stars](/wiki/Star "Star"), which produce nearly all of the light we see from galaxies, as well as interstellar gas in the [interstellar](/wiki/Interstellar_medium "Interstellar medium") and [intergalactic](/wiki/Intergalactic_medium "Intergalactic medium") media, [planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet"), and all the objects from everyday life that we can bump into, touch or squeeze.[[112]](#cite_note-Davies2-113) The great majority of ordinary matter in the universe is unseen, since visible stars and gas inside galaxies and clusters account for less than 10 percent of the ordinary matter contribution to the mass–energy density of the universe.[[113]](#cite_note-114)[[114]](#cite_note-115)[[115]](#cite_note-116)

Ordinary matter commonly exists in four [states](/wiki/State_of_matter "State of matter") (or [phases](/wiki/Phase_(matter) "Phase (matter)")): [solid](/wiki/Solid "Solid"), [liquid](/wiki/Liquid "Liquid"), [gas](/wiki/Gas "Gas"), and [plasma](/wiki/Plasma_(physics) "Plasma (physics)").[[116]](#cite_note-117) However, advances in experimental techniques have revealed other previously theoretical phases, such as [Bose–Einstein condensates](/wiki/Bose%E2%80%93Einstein_condensate "Bose–Einstein condensate") and [fermionic condensates](/wiki/Fermionic_condensate "Fermionic condensate").[[117]](#cite_note-118)[[118]](#cite_note-119) Ordinary matter is composed of two types of [elementary particles](/wiki/Elementary_particle "Elementary particle"): [quarks](/wiki/Quark "Quark") and [leptons](/wiki/Lepton "Lepton").[[119]](#cite_note-Hooft-120) For example, the proton is formed of two [up quarks](/wiki/Up_quarks "Up quarks") and one [down quark](/wiki/Down_quark "Down quark"); the neutron is formed of two down quarks and one up quark; and the electron is a kind of lepton. An atom consists of an [atomic nucleus](/wiki/Atomic_nucleus "Atomic nucleus"), made up of protons and neutrons (both of which are [baryons](/wiki/Baryons "Baryons")), and electrons that orbit the nucleus.[[40]](#cite_note-OpenStax-college-physics-40): 1476

Soon after the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang"), primordial protons and neutrons formed from the [quark–gluon plasma](/wiki/Quark%E2%80%93gluon_plasma "Quark–gluon plasma") of the early universe as it cooled below two trillion degrees. A few minutes later, in a process known as [Big Bang nucleosynthesis](/wiki/Big_Bang_nucleosynthesis "Big Bang nucleosynthesis"), nuclei formed from the primordial protons and neutrons. This nucleosynthesis formed lighter elements, those with small atomic numbers up to [lithium](/wiki/Lithium "Lithium") and [beryllium](/wiki/Beryllium "Beryllium"), but the abundance of heavier elements dropped off sharply with increasing atomic number. Some [boron](/wiki/Boron "Boron") may have been formed at this time, but the next heavier element, [carbon](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon"), was not formed in significant amounts. Big Bang nucleosynthesis shut down after about 20 minutes due to the rapid drop in temperature and density of the expanding universe. Subsequent formation of [heavier elements](/wiki/Metallicity "Metallicity") resulted from [stellar nucleosynthesis](/wiki/Stellar_nucleosynthesis "Stellar nucleosynthesis") and [supernova nucleosynthesis](/wiki/Supernova_nucleosynthesis "Supernova nucleosynthesis").[[120]](#cite_note-Clayton1983-121)

### Particles

The top two rows' columns contain up (u) and down (d) quarks, charm (c) and strange (s) quarks, top (t) and bottom (b) quarks, and photon (γ) and gluon (g), respectively. The bottom two rows' columns contain electron neutrino (ν sub e) and electron (e), muon neutrino (ν sub μ) and muon (μ), and tau neutrino (ν sub τ) and tau (τ), and Z sup 0 and W sup ± weak force. Mass, charge, and spin are listed for each particle.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Standard_Model_of_Elementary_Particles.svg/960px-Standard_Model_of_Elementary_Particles.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Standard_Model_of_Elementary_Particles.svg)

Standard model of elementary particles: the 12 fundamental fermions and 4 fundamental bosons. Brown loops indicate which bosons (red) couple to which fermions (purple and green). Columns are three generations of matter (fermions) and one of forces (bosons). In the first three columns, two rows contain quarks and two leptons. The top two rows' columns contain up (u) and down (d) quarks, charm (c) and strange (s) quarks, top (t) and bottom (b) quarks, and photon (γ) and gluon (g), respectively. Main article: [Particle physics](/wiki/Particle_physics "Particle physics")

Ordinary matter and the forces that act on matter can be described in terms of [elementary particles](/wiki/Elementary_particle "Elementary particle").[[121]](#cite_note-122) These particles are sometimes described as being fundamental, since they have an unknown substructure, and it is unknown whether or not they are composed of smaller and even more fundamental particles.[[122]](#cite_note-PFIp1-3-123)[[123]](#cite_note-Close-124) In most contemporary models they are thought of as points in space.[[124]](#cite_note-125) All elementary particles are currently best explained by [quantum mechanics](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics") and exhibit [wave–particle duality](/wiki/Wave%E2%80%93particle_duality "Wave–particle duality"): their behavior has both particle-like and [wave](/wiki/Wave "Wave")-like aspects, with different features dominating under different circumstances.[[125]](#cite_note-126)

Of central importance is the [Standard Model](/wiki/Standard_Model "Standard Model"), a theory that is concerned with [electromagnetic](/wiki/Electromagnetism "Electromagnetism") interactions and the [weak](/wiki/Weak_interaction "Weak interaction") and [strong](/wiki/Strong_interaction "Strong interaction") nuclear interactions.[[126]](#cite_note-Oerter2006-127) The Standard Model is supported by the experimental confirmation of the existence of particles that compose matter: [quarks](/wiki/Quark "Quark") and [leptons](/wiki/Lepton "Lepton"), and their corresponding "[antimatter](/wiki/Antimatter "Antimatter")" duals, as well as the force particles that mediate [interactions](/wiki/Fundamental_interactions "Fundamental interactions"): the [photon](/wiki/Photon "Photon"), the [W and Z bosons](/wiki/W_and_Z_bosons "W and Z bosons"), and the [gluon](/wiki/Gluon "Gluon").[[122]](#cite_note-PFIp1-3-123) The Standard Model predicted the existence of the recently discovered [Higgs boson](/wiki/Higgs_boson "Higgs boson"), a particle that is a manifestation of a field within the universe that can endow particles with mass.[[127]](#cite_note-OnyisiFAQ-128)[[128]](#cite_note-strasslerFAQ2-129) Because of its success in explaining a wide variety of experimental results, the Standard Model is sometimes regarded as a "theory of almost everything".[[126]](#cite_note-Oerter2006-127) The Standard Model does not, however, accommodate gravity. A true force–particle "theory of everything" has not been attained.[[129]](#cite_note-Weinberg2011-130)

#### Hadrons

Main article: [Hadron](/wiki/Hadron "Hadron")

A hadron is a [composite particle](/wiki/Composite_particle "Composite particle") made of [quarks](/wiki/Quark "Quark") [held together](/wiki/Bound_state "Bound state") by the [strong force](/wiki/Strong_force "Strong force"). Hadrons are categorized into two families: [baryons](/wiki/Baryon "Baryon") (such as [protons](/wiki/Proton "Proton") and [neutrons](/wiki/Neutron "Neutron")) made of three quarks, and [mesons](/wiki/Meson "Meson") (such as [pions](/wiki/Pion "Pion")) made of one quark and one [antiquark](/wiki/Antiparticle "Antiparticle"). Of the hadrons, protons are stable, and neutrons bound within atomic nuclei are stable. Other hadrons are unstable under ordinary conditions and are thus insignificant constituents of the modern universe.[[130]](#cite_note-Allday2002-131): 118–123

From approximately 10−6 seconds after the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang"), during a period known as the [hadron epoch](/wiki/Hadron_epoch "Hadron epoch"), the temperature of the universe had fallen sufficiently to allow quarks to bind together into hadrons, and the mass of the universe was dominated by [hadrons](/wiki/Hadron "Hadron"). Initially, the temperature was high enough to allow the formation of hadron–anti-hadron pairs, which kept matter and antimatter in [thermal equilibrium](/wiki/Thermal_equilibrium "Thermal equilibrium"). However, as the temperature of the universe continued to fall, hadron–anti-hadron pairs were no longer produced. Most of the hadrons and anti-hadrons were then eliminated in particle–antiparticle [annihilation](/wiki/Annihilation "Annihilation") reactions, leaving a small residual of hadrons by the time the universe was about one second old.[[130]](#cite_note-Allday2002-131): 244–266

#### Leptons

Main article: [Lepton](/wiki/Lepton "Lepton")

A lepton is an [elementary](/wiki/Elementary_particle "Elementary particle"), [half-integer spin](/wiki/Half-integer_spin "Half-integer spin") particle that does not undergo strong interactions but is subject to the [Pauli exclusion principle](/wiki/Pauli_exclusion_principle "Pauli exclusion principle"); no two leptons of the same species can be in exactly the same state at the same time.[[131]](#cite_note-132) Two main classes of leptons exist: [charged](/wiki/Electric_charge "Electric charge") leptons (also known as the *electron-like* leptons), and neutral leptons (better known as [neutrinos](/wiki/Neutrino "Neutrino")). Electrons are stable and the most common charged lepton in the universe, whereas [muons](/wiki/Muon "Muon") and [taus](/wiki/Tau_(particle) "Tau (particle)") are unstable particles that quickly decay after being produced in [high energy](/wiki/High_energy_physics "High energy physics") collisions, such as those involving [cosmic rays](/wiki/Cosmic_ray "Cosmic ray") or carried out in [particle accelerators](/wiki/Particle_accelerator "Particle accelerator").[[132]](#cite_note-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134) Charged leptons can combine with other particles to form various [composite particles](/wiki/Composite_particle "Composite particle") such as [atoms](/wiki/Atom "Atom") and [positronium](/wiki/Positronium "Positronium"). The [electron](/wiki/Electron "Electron") governs nearly all of [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"), as it is found in [atoms](/wiki/Atom "Atom") and is directly tied to all [chemical properties](/wiki/Chemical_property "Chemical property"). Neutrinos rarely interact with anything, and are consequently rarely observed. Neutrinos stream throughout the universe but rarely interact with normal matter.[[134]](#cite_note-135)

The [lepton epoch](/wiki/Lepton_epoch "Lepton epoch") was the period in the evolution of the early universe in which the [leptons](/wiki/Lepton "Lepton") dominated the mass of the universe. It started roughly 1 second after the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang"), after the majority of hadrons and anti-hadrons annihilated each other at the end of the [hadron epoch](/wiki/Hadron_epoch "Hadron epoch"). During the lepton epoch, the temperature of the universe was still high enough to create lepton–anti-lepton pairs, so leptons and anti-leptons were in thermal equilibrium. Approximately 10 seconds after the Big Bang, the temperature of the universe had fallen to the point where lepton–anti-lepton pairs were no longer created.[[135]](#cite_note-136) Most leptons and anti-leptons were then eliminated in [annihilation](/wiki/Annihilation "Annihilation") reactions, leaving a small residue of leptons. The mass of the universe was then dominated by [photons](/wiki/Photon "Photon") as it entered the following [photon epoch](/wiki/Photon_epoch "Photon epoch").[[136]](#cite_note-137)[[137]](#cite_note-138)

#### Photons

Main article: [Photon epoch](/wiki/Photon_epoch "Photon epoch")

See also: [Photino](/wiki/Photino "Photino")

A photon is the [quantum](/wiki/Quantum "Quantum") of [light](/wiki/Light "Light") and all other forms of [electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation"). It is the [carrier](/wiki/Force_carrier "Force carrier") for the [electromagnetic force](/wiki/Electromagnetic_force "Electromagnetic force"). The effects of this [force](/wiki/Force "Force") are easily observable at the [microscopic](/wiki/Microscopic_scale "Microscopic scale") and at the [macroscopic](/wiki/Macroscopic_scale "Macroscopic scale") level because the photon has zero [rest mass](/wiki/Rest_mass "Rest mass"); this allows long distance [interactions](/wiki/Fundamental_interaction "Fundamental interaction").[[40]](#cite_note-OpenStax-college-physics-40): 1470

The photon epoch started after most leptons and anti-leptons were [annihilated](/wiki/Annihilation "Annihilation") at the end of the lepton epoch, about 10 seconds after the Big Bang. Atomic nuclei were created in the process of nucleosynthesis which occurred during the first few minutes of the photon epoch. For the remainder of the photon epoch the universe contained a hot dense [plasma](/wiki/Plasma_(physics) "Plasma (physics)") of nuclei, electrons and photons. About 380,000 years after the Big Bang, the temperature of the universe fell to the point where nuclei could combine with electrons to create neutral atoms. As a result, photons no longer interacted frequently with matter and the universe became transparent. The highly redshifted photons from this period form the cosmic microwave background. Tiny variations in the temperature of the CMB correspond to variations in the density of the universe that were the early "seeds" from which all subsequent [structure formation](/wiki/Structure_formation "Structure formation") took place.[[130]](#cite_note-Allday2002-131): 244–266
